one_line_takeaway: A checklist-based comparison of five LLMs shows that default-prompted web auth code from GPT-4o, DeepSeek, Claude, Gemini, and Grok is broadly insecure, none set HTTP security headers or MFA, so human review and security testing remain mandatory before deployment.
key_finding: No LLM produced a comprehensively secure application. Every model scored 0/12 on HTTP Security Headers and none implemented MFA. Only Gemini enforced account lockout; only Grok enforced login rate limiting; only Claude implemented CSRF-token protection and email verification. On the aggregate coverage (Table V): Authentication Security was weakest for all (Grok 3/11 best; DeepSeek and Claude 0/11); Session Security was best for Gemini (8/8) with Claude worst (3/8); Secure Storage was 2/2 for every model except Claude (0/2). Claude uniquely failed Secure Storage (hashing algorithm listed 'NA') and lacked secure-cookie flags and session-fixation protection, so Claude and DeepSeek were the models whose code carried 'Extreme' risk. Grok gave marginally the best authentication/error-handling posture overall, but the authors conclude all outputs require human review and a security-assessment framework before deployment.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a security-compliance checklist for auditing web application code produced by LLMs and applies it in a comparative evaluation of five models (GPT-4o/ChatGPT, DeepSeek v3, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 2.0 Flash Experimental, Grok 3). Using four fixed prompts to generate a PHP/HTML/MySQL e-commerce authentication system, the authors manually score each model's output against ~40 security parameters grouped into six domains (Authentication Security, Input Validation & Injection Protection, Session Security, Secure Storage, Error Handling & Information Disclosure, HTTP Security Headers), and attach a Risk = Likelihood x Impact rating to each parameter. The contribution is the checklist/benchmarking framework plus the cross-model compliance findings, not any new tool, model, or exploit technique.
threat_model: Developers, including non-experts, prompt LLMs to generate deployable web-application (authentication) code and ship it with little or no security review. Because the models omit security controls unless explicitly and exhaustively prompted, the generated code exposes real attack surface: missing brute-force/rate limiting enables credential guessing; absent MFA weakens account protection; missing secure-cookie flags and session-fixation protection enable session hijacking; unescaped input enables XSS/HTML injection; and the universal absence of HTTP security headers (CSP, X-Frame-Options, HSTS) leaves clickjacking, MITM, and XSS defenses off by default. The paper frames risk via a likelihood-times-impact matrix rather than by executing exploits, so the threat is argued at the compliance level, not demonstrated with working attacks.
limitations: (1) Narrow scope: a single application type (e-commerce authentication) in one stack (PHP/HTML/MySQL), so results may not generalize to other languages, frameworks, or tasks. (2) No demonstrated exploitation: risk is derived from a subjective likelihood x impact matrix and a manual Yes/No checklist, not from executing attacks or running SAST/DAST tools, so compliance gaps are not shown to be actually exploitable. (3) Apparent single-generation-per-model with no repetition, temperature variation, or inter-rater reliability, making the Yes/No scores non-reproducible and sensitive to prompt wording (prompts explicitly nudge toward security). (4) The 'NA'/'No' coding is ambiguous, e.g. Claude's 'NA' hashing may reflect that its output did not include the storage component in that prompt round rather than genuinely insecure storage, yet it drives Claude's 0/2 and Extreme-risk rating. (5) Small, non-statistical sample (n=5 models) and reliance on the models' unversioned public interfaces as of March 2025, so findings are a snapshot. (6) The paper acknowledges that fully specifying every security requirement in the prompt can elicit secure code, which limits how much the observed insecurity reflects model capability versus prompt design.

datasets_used: No external dataset; the authors generated their own evaluation corpus by prompting each LLM with 4 fixed prompts to build a PHP/HTML/MySQL e-commerce authentication system
Models evaluated: GPT-4o (ChatGPT), DeepSeek v3, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 2.0 Flash Experimental, Grok 3
Reference standards used as scoring rubric: NIST cybersecurity guidelines and OWASP Top 10
quantitative_results: HTTP Security Headers coverage: 0/12 for all five models (ChatGPT, DeepSeek, Claude, Gemini, Grok)
Authentication Security coverage (Table V): Grok 3/11, ChatGPT 1/11, DeepSeek 0/11, Claude 0/11, Gemini 2/11
Input Validation & Injection Protection coverage: Claude 8/10 (best), Grok 5/10, ChatGPT 5/10, DeepSeek 3/10, Gemini 3/10
Session Security coverage: Gemini 8/8, Grok 7/8, ChatGPT 7/8, DeepSeek 4/8, Claude 3/8
Secure Storage coverage: 2/2 for ChatGPT, DeepSeek, Gemini, Grok; Claude 0/2 (hashing algorithm 'NA')
Error Handling & Information Disclosure coverage: Grok 3/5, ChatGPT 2/5, DeepSeek 2/5, Claude 2/5, Gemini 1/5
MFA implemented by 0 of 5 models; account lockout only by Gemini; login rate limiting only by Grok; CSRF token and email verification only by Claude
Password hashing observed: bcrypt (ChatGPT, DeepSeek, Grok), Argon2 (Gemini), none/NA (Claude); all hashing models used salted hashes
Claude and DeepSeek were the two models whose generated code carried 'Extreme' risk; all models' code carried Very High and (except Grok) High risk

Related-work figures cited (NOT this paper's own results): Fu et al. report Copilot introduced vulnerabilities in 32.8% of Python and 24.5% of JavaScript code; Toth et al. found 11.56% of GPT-4-generated PHP sites compromisable with 26% having at least one exploitable vulnerability; Khoury et al. found only 5 of 21 ChatGPT programs initially secure; over 34,000 CVEs documented in 2024
baselines_compared: Cross-model comparison among the five LLMs themselves (no external tool or human baseline); each model's generated code is scored against the NIST/OWASP-derived checklist rather than against a competing method
